segments.text: If you think good design is just about looks, Pringles is going to change your mind. That perfect chip shape wasn't created by a chef. It was created by a
mathematician. 1950s potato chips had two massive problems. First, they break during shipping and the bags were mostly
air. So, barely any chips. Companies were losing millions, customers were frustrated, and the chip industry needed a miracle. Enter Fred Bore. Not a cook,
not a marketer, a chemist, and a mathematician. What he did next revolutionized snacking forever. He designed a shape called hyperbolic
paraboloid. Sounds complicated, but here's the genius part. Chips stack perfectly. No wasted space, no air,
maximum chips per container. Second, the curved shape is incredibly strong. So,
no more broken chips. And the third reason it's going to blow your mind. The shape is aerodynamic. Chips can now slide smoothly on factory conveyor
belts. And the manufacturing is much more effortless now. So that one mathematical shaped has solved design,
supply chain and customer problems simultaneously. So Fred did not reinvent chips. He just applied maths to make them 10 times better. If you want more amazing design stories, subscribe.
